title: 36yo left shocked at peak tax age realisation
full_text:
You’re not imagining it if you think you’re getting a raw deal on the amount of tax you pay, as a new report reveals the exact ages coughing up the most cash to the taxman.
The major new report by the Actuaries Institute reveals the brutal intergenerational wealth divide in Australia’s $1 trillion tax system, revealing a massive gap between younger workers and retirees.
It comes amid a national discussion about tax fairness sparked by the latest federal budget changes to the CGT and negative gearing.
The report shows two Australians can earn the exact same income, but a 30-year-old will walk away tens of thousands of dollars poorer than a 70-year-old under our current rules.
Report co-author Dr Hugh Miller said the report shows Aussies in the middle of their working lives are being slugged the heaviest.
A radio host has gone after delivering a blistering, 60-second rant against the sheer volume of taxes, levies, and surcharges suffocating everyday Aussies.
“I think 43-year-olds are the magic number. So they on average pay the most tax,” he told news.com.au. “Everything in the report is very much sort of geared towards that sort of average. And we do recognise that at every age there are people that are struggling and need support.
“Taxes are very high in the middle ages. A lot of that is because we tax wages and income, sort of salaries, quite heavily relative to everything else. And so that’s obviously where the tax curve is highest.”
$42k gap in tax for Aussies on the same income
The report looked at average incomes by age, starting with a broad measure of income that includes wage and salary income, capital gains and, for owner occupiers, the value they would otherwise pay in rent.
The analysis reveals that if a 30-year-old and a 70-year-old both pull in a gross income of $100,000, their final financial positions look vastly different. The 30-year-old will see their net income slashed by $14,000 due to taxes. Meanwhile, the 70-year-old will actually receive a net boost of $28,000 from the government via benefits, concessions, and healthcare.
That creates a staggering $42,000 gap in outcomes.
“Once you actually look at the income curve and then layer tax and spend on top of that, you actually get quite interesting and quite different results,” Dr Miller said.
He explained that older Australians often hold their wealth in capital gains and paid-off homes, which are “relatively lightly taxed compared to wages.”
“For somebody aged 30 they are a net taxpayer. But for somebody aged 70, we estimate they have the same income on average once you add those components up. However, they receive from the government on a net basis about $28,000,” he said.
“You end up with an over $40,000 difference in outcomes in terms of their net income.”
He explained why this was the case.
“People are net taxpayers when they’re working, so that’s the working age between 30 to 65,” he said. “And then people are net beneficiaries from the government when they’re young because governments sort of spend a lot on education on average, and they’re also net beneficiaries when they’re older, so when they’re sort of no longer working and paying as much tax.
“Some other costs like age pension and healthcare sort of tend to drive up sort of costs per person.
“It is quite interesting though when we look at something like even superannuation and housing taxes, they actually seem to have the same pattern. So it’s those middle ages that tend to pay the most.
“We found that sort of a little bit surprising, I guess because superannuation the tax mainly happens at the accumulation stage... and housing, I think mainly because middle age people are moving around more than older people, and a lot of our housing taxes relate to stamp duty.
“That sort of tax accrues at that age as well, and that’s I guess in contrast to other types of taxes like the GST which you can see is a lot more evenly spread across the age curve because everyone continues to consume across their lifetime.”
The system ‘isn’t really balanced’
Alarmingly, data shows that over the last 20 years, young adults aged 20 to 30 are the only age group in Australia that has not seen a gain in their net income.
When asked whether he thought the system was currently unfair to those Aussies paying the higher share of tax, Dr Miller said the imbalances were “quite natural”.
“If you think about somebody ageing through their life, there is that natural idea that people will pay tax at some point of their life and there will be net benefits, and so if that is roughly in balance then that’s all okay,” he said.
“Over that life course, it isn’t really balanced at the moment. So if you add up all the tax and all the spend for an individual person and sort of run that through, you end up with a figure that’s about 73 per cent, which means that they’re only paying 73 per cent of the tax on the benefits on the spend they get from the government. So there is actually an imbalance there.
“That balance in large part is made up for the fact that we have sort of a disproportionately larger number of people in that millennial sort of generation cohort at the moment. And so they’re effectively helping fund some of the older ages.”
He said the current system benefits those with quite strong capital gains, so Aussies who have more assets to start with.
“And one thing that comes from that is the fact that the older people have actually seen their incomes increase a lot more than younger people over the last twenty years,” he said. “And so on that basis you’d say, well, we’ve got a system that hasn’t really adapted to that and potentially leading to those sort of slightly different outcomes that we saw.”
‘Australia is relatively lucky’: How migration changes things
Dr Miller said Australia was in a “lucky” position compared to other nations around the world, in part due to a large influx of working age migrants that help pay for the older generations.
“Certainly a lot of the European countries run a much broader base when it comes to GST-type taxes, so consumption taxes. And so those higher rates offer a little bit of protection against the ageing population,” he said.
“Australia is relatively lucky still in a demographic sense. I think part of that is the by-product of relatively high rates of migration that we do actually have a fair chunk of people in that sort of age 20 to 40 category relative to our older population.
“We are a little bit ahead of some of those sort of ageing countries, like I’m thinking of Japan and Europe, where you do see some of those things play out a lot more heavily than we’ve seen to date here.”
What can be done to fix the disparity
With a massive wave of Australians transitioning into retirement over the next decade, the Actuaries Institute warns the current system is unsustainable.
It is calling for a major rethink on age-based tax breaks and how we fund the country.
“Some of that is reviewing some of the age-based tax and spend rules that we do have,” Dr Miller said. “There are quite specific rules that mean there are offsets for certain people once they turn a certain age. Maybe that has to shift a little bit to recognise that people in older ages have that wider range of income and wealth than they used to.
“The Actuaries Institute does have as part of its public policy some better recognition of people’s owner-occupied housing as part of how we think about the superannuation system. And I think that’s quite important because you actually see quite a lot of disparity for people in retirement. The highest poverty rates in Australia are for older people who are on the age pension but don’t own their own home because rent is so punishing.”
He said there were ways we could “tax better” as well and potentially broaden the tax base in terms of the age curve.
“We are very dependent on people aged 30 to 65 to fund our government economy at the moment,” he said. “And so when we think about how we do GST, how we do taxation of investment income... I think there are some real opportunities there.”
Another measure that should be considered is reigning in government spending, he said.
“There probably is a need for fiscal discipline as well,” he said. “Because we do have an ageing population and more people transitioning into retirement, if you compound that with large real increases in government spending, it becomes increasingly difficult to sort of fund that through tax measures.
“Even if we hold per capita spending by age constant, spending will automatically increase because there will be more older people who need higher healthcare needs, for instance, even before you do any policy change.
“The last thing we talk about in the report is supporting asset accumulation because capital gains are quite important in terms of the overall income picture these days, there are genuine equity issues if young people can’t get into the housing market or can’t get into sort of asset accumulation in other forms. So ways to facilitate that are pretty important.”
Bombshell HECS-style plan for older Aussies
The report comes after an independent think tank said much of the discussion about tax fairness was ignoring a bigger problem, who is receiving the most tax benefits.
The Policy Institute Australia (PIA) released a bombshell report suggesting a radical “HECS-style” scheme for older Australians to turn the nation’s bloated and unbalanced welfare system on its head and save $21 billion a year.
Using research and economic modelling, it suggested rebalancing the welfare system to benefit those who need it most with better means testing.
It argued Australia’s safety net is growing faster than the economy, with a rising share of taxpayer support flowing to the most well-off households.
It gave a staggering example of how the current system works.
Treasurer Jim Chalmers and Angus Taylor clashed during question time over the impact of tax reform on the nation's hairdressers, prompting the Liberal leader to accuse his opponent of being jealous of his hair.
One retired couple living in a $5 million Sydney mansion can legally claim a full Age Pension worth $50,000 a year, plus up to $150,000 in aged care support, because the family home is currently exempt from the pension asset test. Meanwhile, the couple next door who rent but hold $1.5 million in superannuation receive absolutely zero pension and roughly half the aged care support.
PIA chief executive Amy Auster warned Australia’s welfare system was breaking down, warning that a failure to evolve how we assess people for payments means more taxpayer money is going to households that have the capacity to pay their own way.
She argued that while the national political debate often rages over income tax cuts, politicians are completely ignoring a bigger problem.
“At the moment, there’s a very big debate about tax changes and the fairness in our tax system. But the tax and transfer system act together,” she told news.com.au. “And if we’re going to talk about fair, who should pay what in tax, we should also be talking about who receives what in transfers.
“What we found is that under the four programs we looked at — child care subsidy, parental leave pay, age pension, and aged care — about $26 billion is going to households that are in the top 20 per cent of Australian households.
“They are the most well-off households by income or wealth, which is a tremendous amount of support that taxpayers are funding to go to Australia’s most well-off.”
Ms Auster said this massive transfer of wealth boils down to incredibly “loose” rules for the rich, which stand in stark contrast to how everyday, struggling Australians are treated when they claim JobSeeker or rent assistance.
“The rules by which we distribute those supports are very loosely tested,” she said. “It’s unlike cash support, like JobSeeker or rent assistance, which has very, very tight means testing and quite a low income cut-off.”
$500k property rule, HECS for retirees
PIA put forward two ideas about how to make the system fairer while saving billions of dollars that could be used to reduce tax in other areas.
The major change comes from a shift in treatment for owner-occupied housing. Under the report’s preferred option, the pension and aged care asset test threshold for homeowners is raised, and any equity in the home above $500,000 is included.
A single retiree in a home worth up to around $1 million would still receive the full pension if that is where most of their wealth sits.
“If you made that change, that affects less than 20 per cent of pensioners,” Ms Auster said. “Some current pensioners would go onto the part pension. Some would come off the pension if they are the owners of quite high-value properties.”
Another idea outlined in the report is to bring in a Retirement Contribution Scheme (ReCS) built on the same principle as HECS.
It said retirees could maintain their current living standards by using what it called a Retirement Contribution Scheme (ReCS) built on the same principle as HECS.
Under the scheme, retirees wishing to defer self-funding their retirement could opt into the scheme and use it to draw an annual income of up to 150 per cent of the pension rate as a loan against their assets.
Like HECS, the loan would be interest-free and indexed to inflation. The loan is repaid only when assets are eventually sold, such as when a retiree chooses to downsize or move into a retirement village, or from their estate. The report estimates about 1 in 20 retirees would be likely to take this up in its first year.
“For a single person, the pension is $31,000, so it would be $31,000 plus another 50 per cent per year. You can draw that down against your asset,” Ms Auster said. “The loan is repaid when income becomes available, which would generally be when assets are sold. For a retiree, that might mean when they sell their share portfolio, it might mean when they move into an aged care facility, or it might be from the estate. There’s no need to repay the loan until it’s appropriate for the assets to be sold.”
She gave an example of how a retiree could top up their cash.
“It would be for any retiree. As an example, we have a cameo of a retiree who currently owns a modest home worth $400,000 and has no other assets,” she said. “This person is eligible for the pension, and under our changes, would remain eligible for the pension.
“But this would give that retiree the ability to top up their pension because they’ve got the home. They could get another $600 a fortnight — $15,000 over the course of a year. By the age of 87, that means another $328,000 in support. The house, because of the increase in equity, is the same on a net basis, and the cost to taxpayers is absolutely minimal.”
Who would lose out
Ms Auster said the report busted two major myths about Australia’s pension system.
“One is that people often see the pension as an entitlement: you work hard your whole life, you pay tax, you get a pension,” she said. “But in fact, we moved away from the pension being an entitlement a long time ago. Forty per cent of current retirees receive no pension at all. The pension is meant to be support for people who are unable to self-fund their own retirement.
“The other myth is that the owner-occupied home is not included at all in consideration of the pension. That is also not true. It’s considered in terms of the other assets that you can hold. Basically, if you have a house, your threshold for your asset test is about $258,000 lower.”
With this in mind, she said the only people who would take any sort of hit from the proposed changes would be wealthy Aussies who would lose an “entitlement coming from taxpayers”, because they would have some drawdown on their assets.
“But it wouldn’t make a difference to their standard of living, because the cash would still be there,” she said.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.

author: Ben Graham
heading: Actuaries
